title: ICLR 2026 Accepted Paper List - Paper Copilot
content: | Statistics | Total | Accept | Poster | Spotlight | Oral | Reject | Withdraw | Desk Reject | Location | Source |. - **Count:** The total number of submissions is calculated as: `#Total = #Accept + #Reject + #Withdraw + #Desk Reject - #Post Decision Withdraw` (when applicable). - **min / max / mean / std:** These are statistical summaries of the *reviewer average scores per submission* within each decision tier (e.g., Accept tier). If rating scores are publicly available, the statistics are based on submissions that opted in for release. - **Withdraw:** Includes papers withdrawn by the authors, including those withdrawn after acceptance. As a result, charts involving author counts (e.g., top authors, affiliations) may contain **redundant entries** if multiple authors share the same name. If you apply filters across multiple columns, the combined match count appears under `“All Filter Matches”`. - **Metrics:** Each paper includes a metrics column showing the review score and citation count from Google Scholar.

title: ICLR 2025 Statistics - Paper Copilot
content: | Statistics | Total | Accept | Poster | Spotlight | Oral | Reject | Withdraw | Post Decision Withdraw | Desk Reject | Location | Source |. - **Count:** The total number of submissions is calculated as: `#Total = #Accept + #Reject + #Withdraw + #Desk Reject - #Post Decision Withdraw` (when applicable). For example, if there are 100 total submissions and 27 were accepted, the `Accept Rate = 27 / 100 = 27%`. - **min / max / mean / std:** These are statistical summaries of the *reviewer average scores per submission* within each decision tier (e.g., Accept tier). If rating scores are publicly available, the statistics are based on submissions that opted in for release. `#Total = #Accept + #Reject + #Withdraw + #Desk Reject - #Post Decision Withdraw`. You can use the `filter box under each column header` to search within these loaded entries. The original default order — based on `review rating`, then `review confidence`, then `title` — is preserved in the *“#”* column.

content: ### News. 3. Two Papers Accepted at ICLR 2026. #### 26 JanTwo Papers Accepted at ICLR 2026. Our group has 2 papers accepted at ICLR 2026. Peng, “ ULTRA-360: Unconstrained Dataset for Large-scale Temporal 3D Reconstruction across Altitudes and Omnidirectional Views”, Proc. on Learning Research, April 2026. “Imagedoctor: Diagnosing text-to-image generation via grounded image reasoning”, Proc. on Learning Research, April 2026. ###### Recent Posts. * Three Papers accepted at MIDL 2026. * Journal Paper Accepted at IEEE Transactions on Image Processing. * Two Papers Accepted at ICLR 2026. * Our Paper is Awarded as Best Paper of PSRC in December 2025. * Journal paper accepted at TMLR.

title: Papers accepted to ICLR 2026 - Yandex Research
content: This work introduces a new scale-wise distillation framework for diffusion models that adapts the idea of next-scale prediction to diffusion-based few-step generators. In this paper, we present a systematic study of NVIDIA’s recently introduced Microscaling formats (MXFP4, NVFP4) for post-training quantization, revealing gaps between their promise and real-world performance. In this paper, we develop the first accelerated, parameter-free gradient method that automatically adapts its stepsize to the local curvature of the objective function at a linear (geometric) rate. In the paper, we present a unified theoretical analysis of SGD with adaptive preconditioning under matrix smoothness and noise, covering popular optimization algorithms such as AdaGrad-Norm, AdaGrad, and Shampoo. In this work, we introduce a parameter-free optimization method based on Sign-SGD. We develop an approach (specifically the ALIAS algorithm) that automatically adapts stepsizes during training to achieve performance competitive with meticulously tuned baselines, effectively eliminating the need for extensive hyperparameter search.

title: 34 papers have been accepted at ICLR 2026 - Riken AIp
content: 34 papers were accepted at ICLR 2026, a major conference on machine learning. * Bayesian Parameter Shift Rules in Variational Quantum Eigensolvers. * Bilateral Information-aware Test-time Adaptation for Vision-Language Models. * CARPRT: Class-Aware Zero-Shot Prompt Reweighting for Vision-Language Model. * Cross-Embodiment Offline Reinforcement Learning for Heterogeneous Robot Datasets. * Diffusion Bridge Variational Inference for Deep Gaussian Processes. * Expert Heads: Robust Evidence Identification for Large Language Models. * Fast Escape, Slow Convergence: Learning Dynamics of Phase Retrieval under Power-Law Data. * Federated ADMM from Bayesian Duality. * Learning from Label Proportions via Proportional Value Classification. * Neuron-Level Analysis of Cultural Understanding in Large Language Models. * Noisy-Pair Robust Representation Alignment for Positive-Unlabeled Learning. * R2-Dreamer: Redundancy-Reduced World Models without Decoders or Augmentation. * Rethinking Policy Diversity in Ensemble Policy Gradient in Large-Scale Reinforcement Learning. * Robust Deep Reinforcement Learning against Adversarial Behavior Manipulation. * Toward Safer Diffusion Language Models: Discovery and Mitigation of Priming Vulnerability. * Towards Understanding Valuable Preference Data for Large Language Model Alignment.

title: GPTZero uncovers 50+ Hallucinations in ICLR 2026 - AI Detector
content: By speeding up the writing process, LLMs and other AI tools are overwhelming scholarly journals and conferences and the peer review pipeline with hallucinated papers ("AI slop"). The GPTZero team used our Hallucination Check tool to scan 300 papers under review by the prestigious International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR). | Title | Average Review Rating | Paper Link | Citation Check Scan Link | Example of Verified Hallucination | Comment |. | Two papers with similar titles exist here and here, but the authors, journal, and date do not match. | Understanding Transformer Architecture through Continuous Dynamics: A Partial Differential Equation Perspective | 3.0 | Understanding Transformer Architecture through Continuous Dynamics: A Partial Differential Equation Perspective | OpenReview |  | Zijie J Wang, Yuhao Choi, and Dongyeop Wei. On the identity of the representation learned by pre-trained language models. | A similar paper with two matching authors exists, but the other authors, title, and journal are wrong.
